params.text:
Recognising a data breach

A personal data breach is any security incident that leads to personal information being lost, destroyed, altered, disclosed or accessed without authorisation. It isn't always a hacker — in care it is often something everyday:

  • A care folder or handover sheet left on a bus or in a car.
  • An email with sensitive details sent to the wrong person.
  • A lost, unencrypted phone or USB stick.
  • Records left open on a screen where a visitor can read them.
  • Talking about a service user where others can overhear.

Reporting a breach promptly

If you think a breach has happened, report it straight away to your manager or the person responsible for data protection — do not try to hide or fix it quietly. Fast reporting limits the harm and lets the organisation act.

Where a breach is likely to put people's rights at risk, the organisation must tell the ICO within 72 hours of becoming aware of it. If there is a high risk to the individuals affected, they must be told too. You are not expected to make that judgement yourself — your job is to raise the alarm quickly so those who can, do.

params.question: Where a personal data breach is likely to risk people's rights, the organisation must report it to the ICO within 72 hours of becoming aware of it.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Actually this is true. Where required, the ICO must be notified within 72 hours, so you must report a suspected breach promptly.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Notifiable breaches must reach the ICO without undue delay and within 72 hours — which is why prompt internal reporting matters.
